schools.summary: Henry: since God anointed Saul king, his power is a stewardship, and this errand is made the express trial of his obedience, aggravating the coming disobedience.
Keil and Delitzsch set the frame: after the Gilgal transgression threatened his throne (1 Sam 13), this war against the long-doomed Amalekites is the test of Saul's obedience.
Gill fixes the occasion, probably at Gilgal, where prophet and king would meet again.
Wesley hears in 'hearken' a call to regain God's favour by exact obedience after his earlier error.
'Lord of hosts' is premised to engage Saul; Amalek were untameable desert wanderers descended from Esau's Amalek, first of the nations to oppose Israel.
Gill: the title of the God of all armies is set first to command Saul's attention.
Wesley: God revenges old injuries on children who continue their parents' practices; it was barbarous to afflict Israel when faint, and impiety against the Lord's throne.
Clarke: only God's absolute authority could justify the exterminating decree; Wesley: the infants' death was a mercy, God being the supreme Lord of life.
Ellicott: the Hebrew 'cherem' (ban) devotes all to God beyond redemption; Israel is only the instrument, like pestilence or the sword, of divine justice and mercy.
Gill (with Kimchi): blotting out Amalek was one of three duties on entering Canaan, reserved for Saul, Israel's first king.

themes: the problem of the innocent slain

schools.summary: Telaim is Telem of Joshua 15:24 in the Negeb; the large numbers suit war on a powerful Bedouin nation; Judah's tenth is strangely small.
Henry: Judah's quota was scandalously short — a twentieth of the host — for 'the day of Judah's honour had not yet come.'
Clarke lays out the divergent numbers of the LXX, Alexandrinus, Complutensian, and Josephus against the Hebrew text.

debates.question: How are the muster numbers and the name Telaim to be read?

sides.position: the Hebrew figures stand; Telaim is the town Telem
the versions vary widely in the numbers
Telaim means 'lambs' — perhaps an open 'place of lambs,' not a town

schools.summary: 'The city of Amalek' was the capital; Keil settles the verb as 'laid an ambush' (from arab), and Ellicott reads 'in a torrent bed,' a dry wady.
Gill: Saul laid wait to intercept the inhabitants, or (per the Targum) set his army in array in the valley.

outliers.note: Preserves a Talmudic legend that Saul's resolve faltered at the dry torrent-bed, recalling the heifer of Deuteronomy 21:4, so that he chose to spare rather than slay.

themes: a rabbinic legend of Saul's misgiving

schools.summary: Henry: Saul acted prudently and piously; God remembers the Kenites' ancestral kindness ('I was hungry and you gave me meat') and separates the precious from the vile.
The Kenites were Jethro's kindred (Numbers 10:29; Judges 1:16), nomads ever friendly to Israel since the Exodus.
Wesley: for their progenitors' kindness all fare the better; when judgments are abroad God separates the precious from the vile (Revelation 18:4).
Gill: sparing the Kenites while punishing Amalek clears the righteousness of God, rewarding one family's kindness as he avenges another's cruelty.

themes: separating the precious from the vile

schools.summary: The sweep ran from Havilah to Shur; 'all' means all who fell into Israel's hands — many escaped, so Amalekites reappear later (1 Sam 27; 30) until Hezekiah's day.
Gill: Saul overcame and slew great numbers of them.
Wesley: he smote all he met from end to end, but a great number fled and secured themselves till the storm was over.
Gill: 'Agag' was a dynastic name like Pharaoh; Saul preserved the king he ought to have killed, the one fatal exception to the ban.
Wesley: all whom he found paid dear for their ancestors' sin, though they were themselves guilty of idolatry and numberless sins.
Keil and Delitzsch: Agag alone was taken alive while the rest that were caught were banned with the sword.
Henry: Saul did his work by halves, giving God the torn and lean while keeping the fat for himself — honouring himself more than God, the fruit of a proud, rebellious spirit.
Keil and Ellicott: they spared the best and destroyed only the refuse; covetousness suggested the choice cattle, pride the sparing of the royal captive.
Gill: Saul spared Agag out of respect, or hope of ransom, or for a triumphal show.
Wesley: they obeyed God only so far as they could without inconvenience to themselves.

sides.position: royal fellow-feeling, or hope of ransom
pride — to parade his royal captive

schools.summary: Gill: the word of prophecy came to Samuel by dream, vision, or articulate voice.
Ellicott: the revelation came very likely in a dream.
Henry: God determines Saul's rejection in secret and acquaints Samuel before a word is spoken to the king — an instance of God's strict justice and man's deceitful heart.

themes: the word of the Lord by night
the doom announced before Saul knows

schools.summary: Gill: not a change of mind or decree, which the unchangeable nature forbids, but a change of dispensation, spoken after the manner of men; grace-gifts are without repentance.
Henry: God 'does not alter his will, but wills an alteration'; the change was in Saul, and Samuel spent the whole night interceding.
Keil and Ellicott: the words express the sorrow of divine love at rebellion, not changeableness; Augustine and Calvin explain both God's 'repentance' and Samuel's grief.
Wesley: repentance implies grief and change of counsel, so it is ascribed to God only when he alters his method; Samuel cried all night for Saul and the people.
'He set him up a place' is literally 'a hand' (yad) — a victory monument, like Absalom's pillar (2 Sam 18:18) — at Carmel in Judah.
Henry: Saul sought his own honour more than God's, boasting of victory when he should have been repenting, marching in pomp to Gilgal.
Gill: sleepless since the night's revelation, Samuel rose early and hastened to meet Saul.
Wesley: the 'place' was a monument or trophy of Saul's victory.
Keil and Ellicott: Saul's 'Blessed be thou... I have performed the commandment' is a feigned welcome masking a bad conscience; in his heart he knew he had been faithless.
Gill: the meeting is at Gilgal.
Henry: the loud profession of obedience is meant to forestall reproof; sinners think to escape judgment by justifying themselves.
Keil: the falsehood lay on the surface; Ellicott (with Lange) hears 'holy humour and cutting irony' as the spared animals convict Saul by their own voices.
Gill: since all living things were to be destroyed, the bleating itself refutes Saul's claim of obedience.
Henry: the cattle are witnesses against him (like the rust of silver, James 5:3); professions are disproved by plain evidence.
Even if meant for sacrifice, Saul too had his own interest (the sacrificial meal); the plea 'to sacrifice to the Lord thy God' is a pious cover for covetous disobedience.
Gill: Saul blames the people as Adam blamed his wife, though nothing was done without his authority.
Clarke: Saul could have restrained the people; plunder had been interdicted so the war might be waged from duty alone.
Keil renders 'leave off excusing thyself'; Ellicott: Saul, turning to go, is arrested by the solemn word and gesture of God's accredited servant.
Gill: 'stay' means both 'stop' and 'suffer me to speak'; Samuel treats the king with courtesy and asks his audience.

themes: the initiative passes to the prophet

schools.summary: Samuel recalls that God raised the once-humble Saul to be head of the tribes; now, flushed with success, he trusts his own strength and disobeys.
Gill: once little in his own sight, Saul is now proud and self-willed.
Clarke turns it to a maxim on prosperity: 'Little in thine own sight — who can bear prosperity?'

debates.question: How is 'when thou wast little in thine own sight' to be construed?

sides.position: a reminder that God exalted the humble Saul, who now disobeys in pride
Kimchi: 'though thou seemest too little to curb the people, thou wast head and shouldst have done thy duty' — rejected as conceding Saul's excuse

themes: the crown as a gift forgotten

schools.summary: Sent on an errand, Saul was bound to execute it; the Amalekites are expressly called 'sinners' (as the men of Sodom, Genesis 13:13), naming the ground of the wrath.
Gill: since the Lord sent him, it was vain to lay the blame on the people.
Henry: the task was so easy and sure it was 'rather a journey than a war,' and Saul would have been no loser by full obedience.

themes: personal responsibility of the one sent
the simplicity of the command broken

schools.summary: 'Didst fly upon the spoil' implies greedy eagerness; Samuel detects grasping covetousness beneath the pious talk.
Gill: Saul disobeyed the very God who made him king and gave him plain and strong orders.
Henry: covetousness is the root, but the sinfulness of sin above all is disobedience — 'Thou didst not obey the voice of the Lord.'

themes: the essence of sin is disobedience
a will unbent to God's voice

schools.summary: Saul breaks in with an emphatic 'Yea, I have obeyed,' offering the captive Agag as proof; Ellicott: he disbelieved in God as heart-reader and cared only to justify himself before Samuel.
Gill: he affirms obedience with utter assurance but gives poor proof of it.

themes: the spared king offered as evidence

schools.summary: Keil: the banned cattle could not be sacrificed, for as most holy they already belonged to God (Leviticus 27:29) and were to be destroyed (Deuteronomy 13:16); the pretext collapses.
Gill: Saul still lays the blame on the people whom, as king, he should have restrained.
Wesley: Saul's conscience begins to awake, but only a little, for he still blames the people.

Keil guards the saying — sacrifices are not worthless, but obedience is greater; Ellicott hears a rapt prophetic oracle echoed through Scripture and by Christ (Matthew 9:13), quoting Irenaeus and Gregory.
Henry: obedience belongs to the eternal law of nature, sacrifice to a positive law; it is harder to bring every high thought into obedience than a bullock to the altar.
Wesley: obedience is a constant moral duty, sacrifice only a ceremonial institution, sometimes needless and sometimes sinful.
Clarke: obedience to God's will is the very end of all religion and its rites.

themes: to obey is better than sacrifice

schools.summary: Ellicott: rebellion, as conscious disobedience, is the deadly sin of idolatry, setting aside the true Master; 'aven' means nothingness/idol, 'teraphim' household gods.
Gill: rebellion against God is as heinous as witchcraft and deserves as sore a punishment.
Wesley: the rejection is a sentence pronounced, not yet an actual deposition, since people and David still owned Saul as king.
Henry: those governed by corrupt inclinations 'consult the teraphim'; disobedience made us all sinners (Romans 5:19) and is enmity to God.

outliers.note: Finding the AV no true translation of the difficult Hebrew, Clarke pairs the nouns — transgression from iniquity, divination from teraphim, rebellion from stubbornness.

themes: the king who will not be ruled

schools.summary: Henry counts several marks of hypocrisy: Saul confesses to Samuel only, excuses his fault while owning it, and cares chiefly to save his credit — unlike David's 'against thee only' (Psalms 51:4).
Ellicott: appalled at ranking with idolaters, Saul yet seeks to justify himself, pleading that he loved men's praise more than God's approval.
Gill: the confession is not ingenuous but made chiefly to get the sentence reversed.
Wesley: it does not appear that Saul plays the hypocrite; he nakedly declares the thing as it was.

debates.question: Is Saul's 'I have sinned' sincere or hypocritical?

sides.position: hypocritical — a cornered king excusing himself and guarding his credit
sincere — an honest confession of the true cause of his sin

themes: against thee only have I sinned

schools.summary: Ellicott: Saul's sorrow is for the earthly consequence — he foresees his power waning and begs the prophet not to desert him; Samuel refuses and turns to depart.
Gill: Saul asks not that Samuel forgive sin against God, but that he intercede and get the sentence reversed — 'pardon' meaning the averting of judgment.
Wesley: charity requires us to believe Saul sincerely desired pardon from both God and man.

debates.question: What lay behind Saul's plea to 'pardon my sin'?

sides.position: self-interest — to keep his standing and avert the penalty
a sincere desire for pardon from God and man

schools.summary: Ellicott (with C. a Lapide): Samuel sees the true springs of Saul's repentance — fear of losing the kingdom and public disgrace — and repeats the divine sentence.
Gill: unsatisfied with Saul's blame-shifting confession, Samuel refuses in indignation, though a good man may later change his mind without a lie.
Wesley: 'I will not return' was no lie, for it agreed with his intention at the time; and rejection from the throne is not rejection from salvation.

themes: changing a stated intent without lying
rejection from the throne, not from salvation
the true springs of Saul's sorrow

schools.summary: Keil: the passive 'it was torn' is correct, an accident not a deliberate act; Ellicott: Saul seized the hem of the 'meil,' the distinctive prophetic dress coming only later (to John the Baptist, Mark 1:6).
Gill: Samuel turned away toward Ramah, meaning to have nothing more to do with Saul.
Henry: Saul, loth to lose the prophet lest the people forsake him too, tears the robe — and Samuel reads the accident as none but a prophet could.
Ellicott: the prophet reads the torn vesture as an omen of the kingdom rent away, and leaves the recipient indefinite — 'a neighbour,' not yet revealed.
Gill: the Spirit impressed the prediction on Samuel; the sentence has gone forth and will not be reversed, like the later rending of the ten tribes (1 Kings 11).
Henry names the 'neighbour better than thou' as David, who would later cut Saul's skirt (1 Sam 24), Saul then owning he would surely be king.
Ellicott prefers 'the Changeless One of Israel' and reconciles it with v11: God does not change; the change is in men, and his predictions are conditional (Malachi 3:6; Psalms 33:11).
Gill: the title grounds why God cannot lie (a mark of weakness) nor repent as men do; some read 'the victory' or 'the eternity' of Israel.
Henry gathers the renderings (Eternity, Victory, the holy One, the triumphant King) and contrasts God's constancy with human fickleness.
Wesley: God cannot lie or repent, for both spring from weakness; he does whatever he pleases by absolute power.

debates.question: How should 'the Strength of Israel' be rendered, and how does 'will not repent' square with v11?

sides.position: 'the Changeless One' — v11's 'repentance' reflects a change in men, not in God
'the Strength' (or Victory/Eternity) of Israel — God cannot lie or change as men do

schools.summary: Ellicott: Saul's penitence is bent on keeping his royal state; he fears losing Samuel's countenance and his crown — 'a true penitent would pray to be humbled, not honoured' (Gregory).
Gill: though now confessing without extenuation, Saul's plea to be honoured shows the repentance is not sincere but hypocritical.
Henry: 'I have sinned, yet honour me' is not the language of a penitent, whose honour is to take shame to himself.

themes: sorrow for standing, not for sin

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott: Samuel returns not to soften the sentence but to preserve the monarchy from revolt and to carry out the ban on Agag that Saul neglected.
Gill: Samuel changed his mind without a lie, returning that Saul might not be despised while king, and to destroy Agag.
Wesley: he turned lest the people withdraw obedience, and to rectify Saul's error by executing judgment on Agag.
Henry: probably by divine direction, to prevent a mutiny and to do justice on Agag; it is not said Samuel presided in the worship.
Henry: Agag came stately or effeminate, thinking the bitterness of death past, having escaped the man of war; many think death far off when it is very near.
Clarke and Ellicott weigh the words: 'delicately' from a root like Eden ('cheerfully/gladly') or 'in fetters'; the LXX reads 'trembling'; most render 'death is bitter.'
Gill: Samuel gave the order to Saul's officers before the assembled people.

debates.question: How did Agag come, and what did he mean by 'the bitterness of death is past'?

sides.position: trembling (LXX), or lamenting that 'death is bitter'

themes: why Saul had spared a king

schools.summary: Keil: Agag forfeited his life by the lex talionis for his own cruelty; the slaying 'before the Lord' was the execution of the ban, an act for God's glory.
Gill: he died justly for his own barbarity as well as his ancestors' sins.
Wesley: God did not punish an innocent son for a father's crime but one who persisted in the same evil; and this is no precedent for private vengeance.
Henry: Samuel acted by special direction from heaven, offering Agag to God's injured justice.

debates.question: Did Samuel slay Agag with his own hand?

sides.position: by an executioner, in his magisterial capacity ('qui facit per alterum, facit per se')
literally, by Samuel's own act, by divine direction

themes: the ban completed by the prophet

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: from this day Samuel broke off all intercourse with the rejected king — not coldness but grief bound by the divine sentence, for he had loved Saul.
Gill: Ramah was Samuel's native place and usual residence.

themes: the parting of prophet and king

schools.summary: 'Came no more to see Saul' is harmonized with 1 Samuel 19:24 — Samuel did not go to Saul, though Saul later came to him; his kingship lost its theocratic relation (Lange).
Wesley: Samuel came no more to visit Saul or seek counsel for him; the later meeting was Saul's coming, not Samuel's.
Gill: 'added not to see him' — he did not go to Saul, though he saw him afterward; Abarbanel says he may have hidden his face.
Henry: we must mourn for the rejection of sinners even when we withdraw from them and though they do not mourn for themselves — 'Jerusalem was secure when Christ wept over it.'
